verses.text: When Jabin king of Hazor had heard, he sent to Jobab king of Madon, the king of Shimron, the king of Achshaph, and to the kings on the north of the mountains, and of the plains south of Chinneroth, and in the valley, and in the borders of Dor on the west, and to the Canaanite on the east and on the west, and to the Amorite, Hittite, Perizzite, and Jebusite in the mountains, and to the Hivite under Hermon in the land of Mizpeh. They went out, they and all their hosts with them, much people, even as the sand that is upon the sea shore in multitude, with horses and chariots very many. When all these kings were met together, they pitched together at the waters of Merom, to fight against Israel. Guidance: The south is fallen, so the north organizes. Jabin (a dynastic name — there will be another Jabin in Judges 4) musters a coalition far larger than Adoni-zedek's: 'as the sand of the sea,' the phrase used of Abraham's descendants in Gen 22:17. The enemy is now numerically what God's own promise once described. And they have iron-age chariots, the technology Israel does not have. The challenge stretches beyond what any infantry could meet.

The LORD said unto Joshua: Be not afraid because of them: for to morrow about this time will I deliver them up all slain before Israel: thou shalt hough their horses, and burn their chariots with fire. Guidance: Three commands in one sentence. 'Be not afraid' — God's standard opening when the odds look impossible. 'I will deliver' — past tense in His vocabulary. 'Hough their horses, burn their chariots' — Israel must dismantle the very technology that scared them. God will not let Israel keep the chariot fleet for later. Trust is required not only in the battle but in the disposal of the weapons. Tomorrow's faith must not depend on yesterday's spoils.

Joshua came, and all the people of war with him, against them by the waters of Merom suddenly; they fell upon them. The LORD delivered them into the hand of Israel, who smote them, and chased them unto great Zidon, and unto Misrephoth-maim, and unto the valley of Mizpeh eastward; they smote them, until they left them none remaining. Joshua did unto them as the LORD bade him: he houghed their horses, and burnt their chariots with fire. Guidance: A long march north, a surprise dawn attack, a chase that swings 100 miles in three directions. Then Israel does the strangest thing of the day — destroys the captured chariots. The text repeats 'as the LORD bade him.' Obedience to the inconvenient command is what proves obedience to the convenient ones.

Joshua at that time turned back, and took Hazor, and smote the king thereof with the sword: for Hazor beforetime was the head of all those kingdoms. They smote all the souls therein with the edge of the sword, utterly destroying them: there was not any left to breathe: and he burnt Hazor with fire. Guidance: Hazor is the chapter's special target. Archaeology confirms it was a massive city in this era — five times the size of Jericho. The 'head of all those kingdoms' must come down hardest. Of all the conquered northern cities, only Hazor is burned. When the head falls, the body cannot rally.

They smote all the souls therein with the edge of the sword, utterly destroying them: there was not any left to breathe: and he burnt Hazor with fire. Guidance: Hazor is the chapter's special target. Archaeology confirms it was a massive city in this era — five times the size of Jericho. The 'head of all those kingdoms' must come down hardest. Of all the conquered northern cities, only Hazor is burned. When the head falls, the body cannot rally.

All the cities of those kings, and all the kings, did Joshua take, smote them with the edge of the sword, utterly destroyed them, as Moses the servant of the LORD commanded. But as for the cities that stood still in their strength, Israel burned none of them, save Hazor only. All the spoil of these cities, and the cattle, the children of Israel took for a prey unto themselves; but every man they smote with the edge of the sword, until they had destroyed them, neither left they any to breathe. Guidance: A practical concession: Israel will move into these cities, so they are left standing — only Hazor burns. Spoil is kept (Achan's lesson absorbed; permission is granted). The judgment is on the people, not the architecture. They inherit walls and wells that other hands built — the goodness of Deut 6:10-11, where God promised them cities they did not build, vineyards they did not plant.

As the LORD commanded Moses his servant, so did Moses command Joshua, and so did Joshua: he left nothing undone of all that the LORD commanded Moses. Guidance: A three-link chain of obedience: LORD → Moses → Joshua → execution. The chapter's quiet thesis verse. The 'nothing undone' is its highest praise — the highest a leader can be praised in scripture. Generations later 1 Kings 8:56 will measure Solomon's day by the same phrase. Faithfulness leaves no item on the to-do list.

So Joshua took all that land, the hills, and all the south country, and all the land of Goshen, and the valley, and the plain, and the mountain of Israel, and the valley of the same: even from the mount Halak, that goeth up to Seir, even unto Baal-gad in the valley of Lebanon under mount Hermon: and all their kings he took, and smote them, and slew them. Guidance: The map is now traced from end to end — Mt Halak in the southern desert (near Edom) to Baal-gad below Mt Hermon in the far north. Every region named in Deut 1:7's original survey is named here, in the same order. Moses' geography lesson is now Joshua's victory inventory. Promise becomes ledger.

Joshua made war a long time with all those kings. Guidance: One understated sentence between two grand summaries. 'A long time' — likely seven years (cf. 14:7-10). The conquest was not a montage; it was a slow daily grind. Even God-given victories have to be lived through. The dust-and-blisters years between the promise and the rest do not get a chapter of their own, only a verse — but they are real time, real fatigue, real faith.

There was not a city that made peace with the children of Israel, save the Hivites the inhabitants of Gibeon: all other they took in battle. It was of the LORD to harden their hearts, that they should come against Israel in battle, that he might destroy them utterly, and that they might have no favour, but that he might destroy them, as the LORD commanded Moses. Guidance: Gibeon stands as the lone exception — and it stands by faith (however deceptive its expression). Every other city closed its gates and chose war. The narrator credits even the hardness of heart to God's overruling, as with Pharaoh. Romans 9 will struggle with this same question. The text does not soften it: the time of the Canaanites' iniquity, foretold in Gen 15:16, has come, and God's restraint has reached its end. The window of repentance — open in Rahab and Gibeon — was open to all and used by two.

At that time came Joshua, and cut off the Anakims from the mountains, from Hebron, from Debir, from Anab, and from all the mountains of Judah, and from all the mountains of Israel: Joshua destroyed them utterly with their cities. There was none of the Anakims left in the land of the children of Israel: only in Gaza, in Gath, and in Ashdod, there remained. Guidance: The Anakim — the very giants whose name made the spies of Numbers 13 lose nerve. Forty years later, their name is recorded as a list of removed obstacles. The text notes a remnant in three Philistine cities: Gaza, Gath, Ashdod. Gath will be where a young shepherd named David grows up to meet one named Goliath. The giants are not all gone — but they are no longer in Israel's land. God removes what blocks the inheritance, even if He leaves work for the next generation.

So Joshua took the whole land, according to all that the LORD said unto Moses; and Joshua gave it for an inheritance unto Israel according to their divisions by their tribes. And the land rested from war. Guidance: Sabbath language at last — 'the land rested.' The same root as God's seventh-day rest in Gen 2. The conquest has reached its weekly evening. The land is taken; the dividing is the work of the chapters ahead. Note 'Joshua gave it' — the human leader hands over what God handed to him. Inheritance is meant to be passed on, not hoarded. A leader's work is finished only when others are settled in what they will keep.
